items.q: What is the difference between voiced and voiceless sounds?
items.correct: Voiced sounds are produced with vocal-fold vibration (/b/, /d/, /g/, /z/); voiceless sounds without it (/p/, /t/, /k/, /s/). You can feel the difference by putting a hand on your throat.
items.misconception: Voiced sounds are loud and voiceless sounds are whispered.
items.random_wrong: Voiced sounds use the lips; voiceless sounds use the tongue.
items.why: Voicing is about vocal-fold vibration, not volume. Whisper has no voicing at all but isn't silent. Students confuse phonation with loudness, missing how voicing distinguishes minimal pairs (pin/bin).

items.q: What is the difference between semantics and pragmatics?
items.correct: Semantics studies literal meaning of words and sentences; pragmatics studies how meaning depends on context, speaker intention and conversational implicature.
items.misconception: Semantics is grammar and pragmatics is vocabulary.
items.random_wrong: Semantics is speech and pragmatics is writing.
items.why: 'Can you pass the salt?' semantically asks ability; pragmatically requests salt. Students miss that pragmatics governs the gap between literal meaning and intended meaning.

items.q: What is the difference between descriptive and prescriptive approaches to language?
items.correct: Descriptivists describe how language is actually used; prescriptivists rule on how language ought to be used — modern linguistics is descriptivist.
items.misconception: Descriptivists describe written language and prescriptivists describe spoken language.
items.random_wrong: Both terms mean the same — analysing language scientifically.
items.why: The split is about attitude, not mode. Descriptivists treat all varieties as valid; prescriptivists make value judgements. Students mistake it for a written/spoken distinction.

items.q: PEEL ends with which letter's job?
items.why: It's Link. “Explain” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.random_wrong: A pull quote in an article
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Inside your own sentence. “A long block quotation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
